title: qstack: Compositional End-to-End Compilation for Fault-Tolerant Quantum Programs
abstract: Compiling quantum programs for fault-tolerant execution requires transforming high-level operations through multiple abstraction layers: from logical gates to error-corrected encodings to hardware-native instructions. A key challenge is that quantum error correction turns purely quantum programs into hybrid quantum-classical programs, where classical feedback from syndrome measurements drives quantum corrections at runtime. Existing compilation frameworks handle these quantum and classical components separately, requiring manual adaptation of classical logic at each compilation stage, all while preserving program semantics. We present qstack, a compiler framework built around a purely quantum intermediate representation in which classical logic is accessed only through opaque callbacks, written in any classical language. The framework's central mechanism, callback wrapping, enables compositional compilation: each compiler pass automatically adapts both quantum operations and their associated classical callbacks, and any kernel dynamically generated by a callback is compiled through the full pipeline. This allows ISA translation and quantum error correction to be expressed as composable compiler passes, including concatenation of error-correcting codes, without manual intervention. We demonstrate end-to-end compilation from a high-level gate set through Clifford gates to trapped-ion native operations, with bit-flip and phase-flip repetition codes, the Steane code, and the Shor code obtained by composing two repetition passes.
summary: This paper introduces qstack, a new compiler framework designed to automate the complex task of composing quantum error correction layers with hardware-specific instructions. By treating classical feedback as opaque callbacks, it allows for a seamless, automated pipeline that handles the transition from high-level logical gates to physical hardware operations. This approach significantly reduces the manual effort required to design and implement multi-layered fault-tolerant quantum programs.
main_problem: The difficulty of composing multiple layers of quantum error correction (QEC) and hardware-specific ISA translations without manual, error-prone adaptation of the classical feedback logic required for syndrome measurement.
main_result: The development of qstack, a compiler framework that uses a callback wrapping mechanism to enable automated, compositional, end-to-end compilation from high-level gates to hardware-native instructions.
method: A kernel-based intermediate representation (IR) where classical logic is accessed via opaque callbacks, which are automatically wrapped by compiler passes to decode measurement results and re-compile dynamically generated kernels.
model_or_system: A fault-tolerant quantum computing framework supporting various gate sets (Toy, Clifford) and error-correcting codes (bit-flip, phase-flip, Steane, and Shor codes) targeting platforms like trapped-ion and neutral-atom hardware.
key_observables: Measurement stack (classical outcomes) and kernel stack (hierarchy of nested kernels and qubit allocations).
main_assumptions: The classical logic is treated as an opaque callback; the framework assumes a LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) discipline for measurements.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows gate definitions; Figure 2 shows a histogram of a repeat-until-success program; Figure 3 illustrates the wrapper structure and runtime data flow; Figure 4 defines the operational semantics; Figure 5 shows a compilation graph.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of hybrid quantum-classical compilation, presents the qstack framework and its callback wrapping mechanism, provides formal operational semantics, demonstrates end-to-end compilation pipelines including concatenated QEC codes, and discusses limitations and future integration with OpenQASM 3.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 2. Distribution of measurement outcomes from 1000 simulation runs of the repeat-until-success program. Bitstrings of length n occur with probability 1/2n, confirming the expected geometric decay.

Fig. 3. Composability of two compiler passes. Left: the wrapping structure after pass 1 and pass 2 each wrap the original callback. Right: runtime data flow when a measurement triggers the outermost wrapper. Decoders run outside-in (pass 2 then pass 1); if the callback returns a kernel, compilers run inside-out (pass 1 then pass 2).
Fig. 5. Compilation graph and example pipelines. Each edge is a compiler pass. QEC passes (loop) preserve the Cliffords ISA and can be applied repeatedly for concatenation.
